flashcards.back: LBA treats the disk as a one-dimensional array of logical blocks numbered 0 to N-1. The OS uses block numbers instead of physical (cylinder, head, sector) addresses. The disk controller handles the mapping internally.

flashcards.front: What is a cylinder on a disk?
flashcards.back: A cylinder is the set of all tracks at the same position across all platter surfaces. For example, track 5 on all 6 surfaces of a 3-platter disk forms cylinder 5. All tracks in a cylinder can be read without moving the head.

flashcards.front: Given S sectors/track and H heads, how do you map logical block B to a physical address?
flashcards.back: Cylinder = B / (S × H), Head = (B / S) mod H, Sector = B mod S. The mapping fills sectors first, then heads (surfaces), then cylinders.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between CAV and CLV?
flashcards.back: CAV (Constant Angular Velocity): disk spins at constant RPM, same sectors per track, used by HDDs. CLV (Constant Linear Velocity): rotation speed varies, outer tracks have more sectors, used by CD/DVD drives.

A hybrid approach used by modern HDDs. The disk is divided into zones — outer zones have more sectors per track than inner zones. Within each zone, rotation speed is constant (CAV). This improves capacity over pure CAV.

flashcards.front: Why is disk scheduling irrelevant for SSDs?
flashcards.back: SSDs have no moving parts — no seek time and no rotational latency. All locations are accessed electronically in roughly equal time, so algorithms that minimize head movement (SSTF, SCAN) provide no benefit.
Track skew offsets the starting sector number of adjacent tracks so that after the head switches tracks, the next sector to read is just arriving under the head — avoiding a full rotation wait.

flashcards.front: How do you calculate total disk capacity?
flashcards.back: Capacity = Cylinders × Heads × Sectors_per_track × Bytes_per_sector. Example: 10,000 × 8 × 200 × 512 = ~7.63 GB.
